response:
devil is a word that does not fit the magical world of Harry Potter.  
The passphrase to enter Dumbledore's office changes frequently throughout the series.  
Students and teachers alike must rely on the Headmaster's whims for access.  
One famous password was treacle fudge which appeared in the first book.  
Another memorable phrase was caput Draconis used later in the story.  
These secret codes protect the private chambers of the wise wizard.  
No single permanent passphrase exists for the entire duration of the saga.  
J.K. Rowling crafted these details to show the dynamic nature of magic.  
Security in Hogwarts relies on enchantments that can be altered at will.  
Trying to guess the current code would be a futile and dangerous effort.  
The true key to the office is often trust rather than a spoken word.  
Dumbledore himself could open the door without ever needing a password.  
Magic remains a mystery that defies simple rules or fixed answers.
